title: Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES)
focus: International agreement regulating wildlife trade, its appendices, and key initiatives.
rationale: CITES is a highly examinable topic for UPSC, especially its legally binding nature, the distinction between its three appendices, and associated initiatives like MIKE and ICCWC.
sectionExcerpt: Voluntary international agreement between governments ensuring international trade in wild animals and plants does not threaten their survival. Legally binding on Parties, but does not take the place of national laws. 185 parties (including India - ratified in 1976). CITES Three Appendices: Based on degree of protection needed. Key Initiatives of CITES: MIKE Programme to monitor illegal killing of elephants; CITES Tree Species Programme (CTSP); International Consortium on Combating Wildlife Crime (ICCWC), 2010.
evidenceBullets: CITES is legally binding on parties but does not replace national laws.
Appendix I covers species threatened with extinction; Appendix II covers species needing trade control; Appendix III covers species protected in at least one country asking for assistance.
Key initiatives include the MIKE Programme, CITES Tree Species Programme, and ICCWC.
CITES publishes the World Wildlife Trade Report.

title: International Union for the Protection of New Varieties of Plants (UPOV)
focus: Plant Variety Protection, Breeder's Rights, and India's non-member status.
rationale: UPOV is crucial for agriculture and IP rights. India's unique stance of not joining UPOV and instead using the PPV&FR Act makes it a prime target for comparative questions.
sectionExcerpt: An intergovernmental organization to provide and promote an effective system of Plant Variety Protection (PVP). Grants Breeder's Rights (an IP right) to incentivize the development of new, improved plant varieties. UPOV Convention 1991: It strengthens breeders' rights and makes the farmer's privilege (to save seeds) optional for member countries. India's Status: Not a member and uses its own sui generis law, the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Authority Act, 2001.
evidenceBullets: Established in 1961 to promote Plant Variety Protection (PVP) and grant Breeder's Rights.
UPOV Convention 1991 strengthens breeders' rights and makes farmer's privilege to save seeds optional.
India is not a member of UPOV.
India uses the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Authority Act, 2001, uniquely protecting both breeder and farmer's rights.

title: Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT) Alliance
focus: Coalition of organizations providing biodiversity data and tools.
rationale: A smaller topic, but the specific coalition members (Birdlife, CI, IUCN, UNEP-WCMC) are factual anchors suitable for a standalone objective question.
sectionExcerpt: A coalition of four organizations: Birdlife International, Conservation International, The International Union of Conservation for Nature (IUCN), and The United Nations Environment Programme - World Conservation Monitoring Centre (UNEP-WCMC). Founded in 2008. Mission: To provide data, tools and guidance that help organisations act on biodiversity-related risks.
evidenceBullets: IBAT is a coalition of Birdlife International, Conservation International, IUCN, and UNEP-WCMC.
Founded in 2008 to provide data and tools for biodiversity-related risks.

title: National Green Tribunal (NGT)
focus: Statutory framework, jurisdiction, powers, and governance of the NGT.
rationale: NGT is a core environmental governance topic. Its legislative framework (the 7 Acts), powers (Civil Court, Natural Justice), and appeal process are frequent UPSC targets.
sectionExcerpt: A Statutory and specialized body aimed at effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to environmental protection. Established in 2010 under the NGT Act. Principal Place of Sitting: New Delhi. Legislative Framework: Water Act, Forest Act, Air Act, Environment (Protection) Act, Public Liability Insurance Act, Biological Diversity Act. Powers: Has power of a Civil Court... Guided by Principles of Natural Justice. Members: Hold office for 5 years, but shall not be eligible for re-appointment. Appeal: Orders are binding but not final (appeal could be made in SC within ninety days).
evidenceBullets: Established in 2010 under the NGT Act as a statutory body for environmental cases.
Principal sitting place is New Delhi, with four others: Bhopal, Pune, Kolkata, Chennai.
Jurisdiction covers 7 Acts including Water Act, Forest Act, Air Act, EPA 1986, Public Liability Insurance Act, and Biological Diversity Act.
Has powers of a Civil Court but is guided by Principles of Natural Justice and not bound by the Code of Civil Procedure or Indian Evidence Act.
Members hold office for 5 years and are not eligible for re-appointment.
Appeals against NGT orders can be made to the Supreme Court within 90 days.
